speaker: interviewer
text: [? Maybe ?] [? you ?] [? want ?] to go [INAUDIBLE]. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I hear [INAUDIBLE]. [BACKGROUND NOISE] How's it going? OK. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You were telling me about when came to this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All right. When-- after we come to-- we come to New Orleans, and then we come to Denver, Colorado. We stayed two days in New Orleans. They send us to Denver, Colorado. In Denver, Colorado, the-- the next day they took us to the Jewish Agency. The Jewish Agency, there they told me-- Albert, he says, you have to come at least six months to one year to become-- to learn to speak English before we can get you a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, you're kidding. I'm not going to wait that long. What am I going to do home? He said, well, he says, we don't have no choice. Are you going to-- are you going to communicate with the people? How, you know, if you don't speak the English?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to my wife, we come-- we-- we arrive here by six o'clock in the morning. We-- they put us in a hotel. They call it the Moscow Motel in Denver, Colorado. It was about 10 miles away from the city. So-- so I took the bus. I took the bus, you know, and I sit there. The bus-- the-- the guys-- the guy-- the bus driver's calling me. [POINTING] I don't know what he was talking about. In Europe, you-- you put the more-- they come, the bus. And they give you the-- take the money and they sit. I don't knew that. Then finally stopped bus and he comes to me angry. He says, if you don't pay, tell me in English. I-- I say, well, I'm giving them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh no, he says, you put it here in the-- in the little thing. OK? And then I learned something new. In America you have to put the money inside there. Because over there, they give you-- they give you a ticket. They come to-- right to your seat. So after that [? done ?], when I said-- went downtown in the city of Denver, running around, back and forth, back and forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: It started getting dark. I took the key with me, in the hand, from the-- from the place. I don't want to get lost in case I want to go home. I had to. Then I marked the place where I left, when I got out of the bus. They call it Champa St. I remember that. That was-- so I have to look around, see how the city looks here, in America here. And I had a-- I have a leather jacket. The-- like the [? flyers ?] jacket. Because I used to ride a motorcycle. And-- and I used to wear the jacket. You know, I was a motorcycle rider. When I used to cross countries, I used to go with a motorcycle, fast. So I step in the corner, and I'm waiting around, looking for the bus to come. All of sudden, you won't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: A police car stopped and then grabbed me right off the bus. I thought, what the hell is going on here? So they grabbed me, they-- and tried to push me around, to handcuff me. And I was struggling with them. So finally they handcuffed me. And I said, what's happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started talking to him in Spanish, German, Italian. Nobody understands nothing. That took me from there. And they took me about three, four blocks and in a place, a Woolworth grocery, drugstore called Woolworth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Took me there and they put me in the corner over there. And they're watching me. And the-- the guy had a-- had the-- the hand on the gun. What the heck's going here? What's going on? So-- so I wait and I wait. And then-- then all of a sudden, a-- a guy come to me. And he says to-- he-- he says to me, what language you speak? I say, I speak Spanish, French, German, Greek, Yiddish, Hebrew. He-- he says to me, Spanish. OK. Say, why you got-- why they arrest me? You make a holdup in Woolworth's store here. And we're waiting for the manager to-- to identify you. He tell me, a guy, in Spanish. Me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I just got gassed here from Europe. This morning we arrive here, from New-- from New Orleans. He says to me, well, he says, they think you-- you're the guy with the leather jacket, the stocky guy, muscular guy. Said, you guys crazy! Said, I got my wife and my kid waiting in the hotel for me. He says, well, the manager's going to come here. Then after an hour, come the manager from the drug-- grocery-- from the drug store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he come to me. He looks. He says, yeah. This is the guy. I went to him, I say, it's me?

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked me in the eyes. Oh, no, no, he says. The other guy had black eyes. You got blue eyes. He says, no, it's not him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So because of my eyes, then they let me go. Then I said to-- to the-- to a Spanish guy, I don't know what I am. I say, I want to go home. My wife is waiting for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, she's going to worry. Just-- When. I said to her, the policeman said, oh well, I'm going to take you to the-- to the corner over there, where I pick you up. Then I said, I don't know what kind of bus to take. He says, you wait here. And I was lucky, I pick up the last bus. It was 12 o'clock at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the time I went home, my wife was on the-- on the door, and waiting and crying. What happened to you? And I tell her what. And she say, I couldn't believe it. This is the first [INAUDIBLE] the United States. So the following day, I got up again. I went-- I went to see where the manufacturers are. And I saw two guys picking up sofas and loading into a truck. I went to them. And I started talking to them. This is the second day I was in the United States. I talked to him and-- and I start to-- then it turns to be there are two Mexicans. I spoke to him and I says, [SPANISH] por favor? You know, I want a job here. I'm looking, you know, for a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says to me, well, he says, the boss is Judeo. [SPANISH]. It means, the boss is a Jew. I don't know if he's going to take you. I say, I can talk to him. I figure if he's a Jew, probably speak Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to him. He turns to be, was a Russian Jew. Born in Russia by a factory. And the name was [? Landy ?] Manufacturing, upholstery. So I went and I start talking to him. And I say, in Yiddish. He says to me, but you-- you don't speak English. How you can come? But I speak Spanish. I can work with the Mexicans. I can speak Spanish. He says to me, the only-- the only available job around here is, right now, he says you are to be a janitor. What's a janitor? You're going to sweep, you're going to clean the toilet, you're going to clean there. You're going to-- So what am I going to do? I need the money. I say, I've only got $5. I have to support my family. So OK. Come tomorrow, you got a job. So I got a job there, cleaning the-- the place over there. Anything they give to me. Well, I can't be no particular.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, where to eat and everything. The Jews actually said to me, you come next month and we're going to give you another check. I got only one check from them. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I work there. Start working around, left and right. Then after a while, so-- was a-- an older lady. After two or three months there, I was working there. Was another woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she spoke German. And she was an upholstery cutter. But she was in the 60s. So I saw her there.

speaker: interviewer
text: As she was trying to pick up a roll, a plastic roll, weighed around 70, 75 pounds. She was struggling. And I said to the Mexican next to me, I said, what kind of-- [SPANISH]? What kind of gentleman? Means what kind of gentleman? You see a lady struggling, don't you give her a hand? He says, to hell with her. She makes three times more money than I do. Why should I help her?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the answer he give me, the Mexican guy. I said, well you-- you-- you're not a gentleman. I said, but I'm a gentleman. I go. I go and I pick up the lady. The lady says to me, just help me put them in the-- in the elevator. What It was the bottom floor to go to the third floor. I said, no, I take it to your place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I put the roll on my shoulder, you know, 75 pound mean nothing to me at that time. I was in the early 20s, you know. Pick up and I took it. Oh, she thank me a million times.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, listen. I said, any time you want some help, I'm not like those guys. I'm a European, and I respect women. I say-- I say you just call me and I'll be there to help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so she got a-- a like to me. You know, she-- she started to liking me. You know? It's like she was-- she was looking like a son. I was in the early 20s. Like I said, she was in the early 60s. So after a while, she saw I was helping her all the time. She went to the boss and [? he ?] says, Mr. [? Landy, ?] [? he ?] says, you know, [? he ?] says, you pay me three times more than those helpers over there. And I lose too much time to go bring the rolls and everything. Why don't you put Albert to help me? You know, that way, you know, he gets load wages. That way I can produce more when I'm in the-- in the thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, all right. So she took me. And I was an-- a apprentice. you know, a helper.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I help in there. She was showing me every trick in the business. And I catch fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years later, I was the manager there. The-- those guys used to make fun of me, those Mexicans. Hey, clean the toilet good in Spanish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say you talk too much. I'm going to throw you out. The same guys that used to make fun of me, you know? You know, so-- so I worked there for 9 and 1/2 years, in this place. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I become the supervisor. And then from there, we come to-- my kids want to see, so bad, Disneyland. We drove down from LA here, to see Disneyland. My wife was so crazy. She liked Los Angeles. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 1959. So we went there. We saw whatever we had. And she liked the ocean, blah, blah, blah. You know? And then-- then it's not so cold. Like here, like in Denver. So we moved to LA. So in LA, you know, I start-- I start working, also, the same line of business. Then I used to work all night in upholstery, in the garage. [? That ?] [? anybody ?] [? who ?] [? wishes ?] [? to ?] [? complain, ?] doing recoveries and everything, making extra money, blah, blah, blah. You know? That way I accumulate enough money to buy myself a business.

speaker: interviewee
text: I bought a business there, on the market, and 305 Oceanfront in Venice Walk. So turns to be it was a gold mine. Work hard, but we make good money there. We got rich. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So-- so there, you know, it's a lot of stories we have to tell about this place, over there. So there, there after 20-something years, you know, we got tired. So we sold it. And we retire. Now we're taking cruises and blah, blah, blah, you know. that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell me about your kids, when they were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Born--

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest daughter was born in Austria. My son, Andy, was born in Denver, Colorado. And Yvette, she was born in-- in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regina.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's going to be 47 years old soon. Andy's going to be-- he's 42. He'll be 43. And Yvette is 30 years old, she's going to be 31 soon. And I have a grandchild.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Danielle. She is 9-and-1/2-years old now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betty Rosa. She is a good woman. She helped me a lot in the store and everywhere else. She is an angel. All the people that told her not to marry me, they're all-- the people, they are all divorced. And I'm still with her, 48 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mazel Tov. Is there anything that you'd like, any message you'd like, to leave for your children or grandchildren, future generations? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went like the lambs. And my-- and when I was a youngster, I lost my teenage years. You know? I never saw teenager. From a child, I grew up to an adult. You know? And it teach me to be very vicious when I was young. Life didn't mean nothing to me at the time. Every time I used to go for a mission, I don't care if I was going to kill-- get killed, or to kill somebody. Because I was-- I grew up in this kind of life. See people getting killed left and right, all the time, by the thousands, every day. Then for years, I used to bury them myself. I was working a job like that, burying thousand of peoples.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they just watch, you know? Not to let it go, like the way we did, like the lambs, to learn not to fight back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Any-- anybody will hit you. You slap a little bit, you knock in the mouth, you knock the teeth out. If he knocks your teeth out, you kill him. Don't let nobody push you around. Because if you want to push-- you should stand on your feet and learn how to fight. Not to be a violent person. I mean somebody pushes you, then you push back. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you want me to tell the story in the store where the three Nazis walk in, in my store? Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: One time-- one time there were three Nazis in the store. We had Kosher salami and Kosher Bologna written down on the sign over there. And they were laughing. Started making fun of it, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: I went around the counter with a baseball bat. And I tell them to get out. They start laughing, you know? And then before you know it, some-- one of them raised their hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hit one over the head and knock his hat off. Then I bust all three of them with a baseball bat. You know, then the ambulance come over. They took them away. When I-- I was very good friends with the police. I used to feed them sandwiches and everything. And they told me all the time, in case you going to hit somebody, you say, he threw the first punch. You were doing it in self defense. And that's what I did. They don't do nothing to me. I send-- I send him to the hospital. I-- blood-- bloody everywhere. And when one was leaving, taken away, he says to me, I come back and I get you, little Jew! I say, you walk through the door and I blow your head off. You wouldn't even-- I won't even give you a chance to come inside. Your head, you're going to find it across the street. And that was the end of it. I never saw them again. [BACKGROUND NOISE] There's a lot of stories from the store, millions of them. Now, you know, if you know Venice Beach, you know what kind of neighborhood I was. They gave me a nickname, the King of Venice. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's what they used to call me. Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my beautiful wife, Betty Rosa. I'm married to her 48 years. And there's my son, Andy Rosa. And I know him for 42 years. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something about your dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is everything to me. He's been-- been the biggest influence in my life. He brought me up with good values, Jewish values. Made me very proud to be a Jew. And he's instilled in me the idea that what had happened to him won't ever happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mrs. Rosa, would you like to say something about--

speaker: interviewee
text: What should I say? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I told her about-- about the people who say not too marry me, I was a troublemaker. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, my husband, I love him very much. And we've been a long time together. And through-- through bad times and good times. And I have three beautiful children. And-- and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is me in Germany. Sometimes in 1945. I don't remember, exactly, the date. The butch you see there-- [LAUGHS] I-- I-- I-- I took it-- I took it from the German officer. I had no shoes that time. And I took it from him. And the belt, I took it from him. Now I got shoes and belt from Germany. OK. I'm there. It's me, on the right side [INAUDIBLE] from the fire. And there was one other transport they were bringing up from Poland to there. To the English-- the American zone. And from there, they was going to go to Palestine. It was sometimes in 19-- close to 1946. Probably early, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Germany, someplace. It was in a forest. OK, on the left side is my wife, Betty Rosa. The baby is my daughter, Regina Rosa. And I'm on the right. This, the picture is when I-- when I got out of the prison and I come to Salzberg, to get my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes in 1947. No, no, no. In '48. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is me again. My wife is on the left side. And I'm on the right. And there's a picture we took at sometimes in the beginning of 1948. Before Israel become a state.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in-- in somewhere in Austria, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. And what does say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it says to go-- to go to Israel. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is a card taken in 1948, at New Year's. And we see you in Israel, in the future. Close enough? This is my daughter, Regina, graduated from the high school. She's a very pretty girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was in 1968.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hamilton High School, West LA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Andy, also graduate from the high school in-- in California, in Hamilton High School.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1971. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my daughter, Yvette. She graduated from high school in Bloomington High School in the valley, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1962? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in 1962.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was graduated in 1982. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my son's bar mitzvah, Andy Rosa. He had the bar mitzvah in 1966.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: In California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's me, congratulate him. He become a man. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my daughter, Regina, and her daughter, Danielle, my granddaughter. She lives here in-- in the valley, not too far from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: About, I will say, a couple of years ago. So it's maybe three years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 1992 or '93.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a document about-- about the-- the-- to get pension from Germany. I had to go to a German doctor to examine me. And he find the things, you know, what happened to me in the camp. Also he wrote down about my brother, when he got hang, and the wounds I have in my body, or after he fully examined me.
